title: Water potential
summary: Water potential is the single idea that lets you predict, without guessing, which way water will move between any two cells or solutions. This Higher Level lesson defines water potential $\Psi$ as the tendency of water to move, fixes pure water at 0 kPa as the reference, and splits $\Psi$ into its two components — the always-negative solute potential $\Psi_s$ and the pressure potential $\Psi_p$ — combined as $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$. From there it treats osmosis as flow down a water-potential gradient, the responses of plant cells (turgid, flaccid, plasmolysed) and animal cells in hypertonic, hypotonic and isotonic solutions, and how to predict the direction of net water movement from $\Psi$ values. Every worked example is laid out the way our marking engine actually awards Paper 2 marks — method marks, answer marks and error-carried-forward — so you can see how showing your working protects your score.
learningObjectives: Define water potential $\Psi$ as the tendency of water to move, measured in kPa, with pure water at atmospheric pressure set to 0.
Distinguish the solute potential $\Psi_s$ (always negative) from the pressure potential $\Psi_p$, and combine them using $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$.
Explain osmosis as the net movement of water from higher (less negative) to lower (more negative) water potential.
Describe the behaviour of plant cells (turgid, flaccid, plasmolysed) and animal cells in hypertonic, hypotonic and isotonic solutions.
Predict the direction of net water movement between cells and solutions from their water-potential values, showing working the way marks are awarded.

simpleExplanation.title: Which way will the water go?
simpleExplanation.summary: Water potential ($\Psi$) is a number that tells you how ready water is to leave a place. Pure water at ordinary pressure is the zero mark; dissolving anything in it pulls the number below zero. Compare the numbers for two neighbouring places and water always drifts from the higher (less negative) one to the lower (more negative) one.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture water potential as height on a hillside, but the whole hill sits below sea level so every reading is a negative number. Pure water is the shoreline at 0. Dissolve salt or sugar and you dig the ground lower — the more solute, the deeper the pit. Squeeze the water (as a plant's cell wall does when the cell is full) and you push the ground back up. Water, like a ball on a slope, always rolls downhill: from the shallower pit to the deeper one, that is, from higher $\Psi$ to lower $\Psi$. It stops rolling only when both spots sit at the same level.
steps: Start from the reference: pure water at atmospheric pressure has $\Psi = 0$ kPa. Everything real in a cell is more negative than this.
Add solutes and the solute potential $\Psi_s$ goes negative — the more concentrated the solution, the more negative $\Psi_s$.
Add or feel pressure and the pressure potential $\Psi_p$ changes — a turgid plant cell's wall pushes back, giving a positive $\Psi_p$.
Combine them: $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$. Then compare two regions — water moves from the higher $\Psi$ to the lower (more negative) $\Psi$ until they are equal.

flashcards.back: A measure of the tendency of water to move out of a system by osmosis. Water moves from higher (less negative) to lower (more negative) water potential. Measured here in kilopascals (kPa).
Pure water at atmospheric pressure is defined as $\Psi = 0$ kPa. Because solutes lower it, real solutions and cells almost always have a NEGATIVE water potential.
The component of water potential due to dissolved solutes. It is ALWAYS negative (or zero for pure water): more solute means a more negative $\Psi_s$. Also called osmotic potential.

The component of water potential due to physical pressure. It is positive in a turgid plant cell (the wall pushes in), roughly zero in an open solution or a flaccid cell, and can be negative under tension.
$\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$: total water potential equals solute potential plus pressure potential. In an open solution $\Psi_p = 0$, so $\Psi = \Psi_s$.
Net water movement is always from the region of HIGHER (less negative) water potential to the region of LOWER (more negative) water potential, until the two are equal.

flashcards.front: Which is higher: −400 kPa or −650 kPa?
flashcards.back: −400 kPa is HIGHER (less negative). Water would move from the −400 kPa region to the −650 kPa region. 'Higher $\Psi$' means closer to zero, not larger in magnitude.

A plant cell that has gained water in a hypotonic solution. The vacuole swells and the membrane presses on the cell wall, so $\Psi_p$ is large and positive. The wall prevents bursting; turgor supports the plant.
A plant cell in an isotonic (or slightly hypertonic) solution with no net water gain. The membrane no longer presses on the wall, so $\Psi_p \approx 0$ and the tissue is limp.
A plant cell in a strongly hypertonic solution: so much water leaves that the membrane pulls away from the cell wall. This is plasmolysis, and it can be lethal.

flashcards.front: Animal cell in a hypotonic solution
flashcards.back: Water enters by osmosis and, with no cell wall to resist the swelling, the cell may burst (lysis / haemolysis for a red blood cell).

flashcards.front: Animal cell in a hypertonic solution
flashcards.back: Water leaves by osmosis and the cell shrinks and shrivels (crenation in a red blood cell). In an isotonic solution there is no net movement and the cell keeps its shape.

flashcards.front: Why plant cells do not burst but animal cells can
flashcards.back: The rigid cellulose cell wall generates a rising pressure potential as water enters; when $\Psi_p$ makes the cell's $\Psi$ equal to the outside, net inflow stops. Animal cells have no wall, so nothing halts the inflow before lysis.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: 'water moves to where it is more concentrated'
flashcards.back: State it in water-potential terms: water moves toward LOWER (more negative) water potential — i.e. toward the more concentrated solution. Never write that water moves to higher water potential.

quickCheck.prompt: Cell X has $\Psi = -300$ kPa; neighbouring cell Y has $\Psi = -550$ kPa. In which direction is the net movement of water?
options: From Y to X, toward the higher water potential
From X to Y, from higher to lower water potential
There is no net movement because both are negative
From X to Y, from lower to higher water potential
quickCheck.answer: From X to Y, from higher to lower water potential

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement about solute potential ($\Psi_s$) is correct?
options: It is always negative (or zero for pure water)
It is always positive because solutes add energy
It equals zero in every living cell
It becomes less negative as more solute is added
quickCheck.answer: It is always negative (or zero for pure water)

quickCheck.prompt: An open beaker of solution has $\Psi_s = -450$ kPa. What is its total water potential $\Psi$?
options: +450 kPa, because pressure is added
0 kPa, because it is a solution
−450 kPa, because $\Psi_p = 0$ in an open container
It cannot be found without the temperature
quickCheck.answer: −450 kPa, because $\Psi_p = 0$ in an open container

quickCheck.prompt: A plant cell is placed in a strongly hypertonic solution. What is the most likely outcome?
options: It becomes turgid as water enters
It bursts because there is no cell wall
It becomes plasmolysed as water leaves and the membrane pulls from the wall
Its solute potential becomes positive
quickCheck.answer: It becomes plasmolysed as water leaves and the membrane pulls from the wall

quickCheck.prompt: A red blood cell (animal cell) is placed in pure water. What happens and why?
options: It shrivels, because water leaves down the water-potential gradient
It swells and may burst, because water enters and there is no cell wall to resist
Nothing, because the solutions are isotonic
It becomes turgid, because its pressure potential rises like a plant cell
quickCheck.answer: It swells and may burst, because water enters and there is no cell wall to resist

faq.q: Why is solute potential always negative?
faq.a: Pure water is defined as the zero point. Dissolving a solute lowers the tendency of water to leave, so the water potential contributed by solutes falls below zero. Because you can only ever add solute (never 'un-dissolve' below pure water), $\Psi_s$ can be zero at best and is negative for any real solution. The more concentrated the solution, the more negative $\Psi_s$ becomes.

faq.q: What is the difference between water potential and osmosis?
faq.a: Osmosis is the process — the net movement of water across a partially permeable membrane. Water potential is the quantity that tells you which way that process runs and when it stops. Instead of vaguely talking about 'water concentration', you compare two $\Psi$ values: water moves from higher to lower until they are equal. Because $\Psi$ also includes pressure, it predicts water movement in turgid plant cells that a solute-only argument cannot.

faq.q: How can a turgid plant cell stop taking in water even though it sits in dilute surroundings?
faq.a: As water enters, the cell contents push the membrane against the rigid cell wall and the wall pushes back, raising the pressure potential $\Psi_p$. This positive $\Psi_p$ makes the cell's overall $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$ less negative. When $\Psi_p$ has risen enough that the cell's $\Psi$ equals the surrounding $\Psi$, there is no gradient and net inflow stops — the cell is turgid, not burst. Animal cells lack this wall-generated pressure, which is why they can keep taking in water until they lyse.

faq.q: Why do we sometimes see MPa and sometimes kPa in textbooks?
faq.a: They are the same quantity in different units: 1 MPa = 1000 kPa, so $-0.4$ MPa is $-400$ kPa. This lesson works in kPa. Whichever unit a question uses, keep it consistent throughout your working and never drop the minus sign, because the sign is what tells you the direction of water movement.

sections.content: Ask a plant cell whether it will gain or lose water and it will not answer with 'concentration' — it answers with water potential. Water potential turns the loose idea of osmosis into a single signed number you can compare between any two places, so that predicting the direction of water movement becomes arithmetic rather than intuition. This Higher Level lesson builds that number from the ground up: the reference point of pure water, the two components that shift it, the equation that combines them, and the way real plant and animal cells behave in solutions of different strength. Because D2.3 questions mix short calculations with explanations, every worked example here is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — so you can see how showing each step protects your score.
Defining water potential

Water potential, written with the Greek letter psi ($\Psi$), is a measure of the tendency of water to move out of a system by osmosis. It captures, in one number, how 'free' the water is to leave. The reference point is fixed by convention: pure water at atmospheric pressure has a water potential of exactly 0. We measure $\Psi$ in units of pressure — this lesson uses kilopascals (kPa). Because dissolving solutes lowers water's tendency to move, and cells are full of solutes, the water potential of a living cell or a real solution is almost always a NEGATIVE number. The whole predictive power of the idea comes from one rule that never changes: water moves from higher (less negative) water potential to lower (more negative) water potential.

items: $\Psi$ measures the tendency of water to move out of a system by osmosis.
Pure water at atmospheric pressure is the reference: $\Psi = 0$ kPa.
Real solutions and living cells have NEGATIVE water potential.
Net water movement is always from HIGHER (less negative) to LOWER (more negative) $\Psi$, until the two are equal.

sections.content: The two components: solute potential and pressure potential
Two independent factors set a cell's water potential, and the equation simply adds them together:
\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p

The solute potential $\Psi_s$ (sometimes called osmotic potential) is the contribution of dissolved solutes. Because solutes can only lower water's tendency to move relative to pure water, $\Psi_s$ is always negative — the more concentrated the solution, the more negative it becomes. The pressure potential $\Psi_p$ is the contribution of physical pressure on the water. In an open beaker of solution there is no extra pressure, so $\Psi_p = 0$ and the solution's $\Psi$ is simply its $\Psi_s$. Inside a plant cell, water entering makes the contents press outward on the rigid cell wall; the wall pushes back and generates a positive pressure potential (turgor). Under tension, as in the xylem of a transpiring plant, $\Psi_p$ can even be negative.

items: **Solute potential $\Psi_s$:** contribution of dissolved solutes; ALWAYS negative (0 for pure water); more solute → more negative.
**Pressure potential $\Psi_p$:** contribution of pressure; positive in a turgid plant cell, ≈ 0 in an open solution or flaccid cell, negative under tension.
**Combine them:** $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$. In an open solution $\Psi_p = 0$, so $\Psi = \Psi_s$.

sections.content: The most common slip in this whole topic is treating 'higher' as 'bigger number'. On the number line, −350 kPa is HIGHER than −500 kPa because it is closer to zero. Water flows toward the more negative value. Sketch a quick number line if you are unsure — it prevents the single most-penalised error in D2.3.
Osmosis as movement down a water-potential gradient

Osmosis is the net movement of water across a partially permeable membrane, and water potential tells us exactly which way it runs. Rather than argue about 'where the water is more concentrated', we compare two $\Psi$ values and let water move from the higher to the lower. The flow continues until the two water potentials are equal, at which point there is no net movement — the system is at equilibrium (though water molecules still cross the membrane in both directions at equal rates). Because $\Psi$ folds pressure in alongside solutes, this single rule works even where a solute-only account fails, such as a turgid plant cell that has stopped taking in water despite sitting in dilute surroundings.
Plant cells: turgid, flaccid and plasmolysed

Place a plant cell in a HYPOTONIC solution — one with a higher (less negative) water potential than the cell — and water enters by osmosis. The vacuole swells and pushes the membrane against the cellulose cell wall, which resists and generates a positive pressure potential. As $\Psi_p$ rises, the cell's overall $\Psi$ becomes less negative until it matches the surroundings and net inflow stops. The cell is now TURGID, firm and supportive; the wall prevents it from bursting. In an ISOTONIC solution there is no net movement and, with the membrane no longer pressing hard on the wall, the cell is FLACCID and the tissue limp. Place the cell in a strongly HYPERTONIC solution — lower (more negative) water potential than the cell — and water leaves. So much water can be lost that the membrane peels away from the cell wall: this is PLASMOLYSIS, and severe plasmolysis kills the cell.

items: **Turgid (hypotonic surroundings):** water enters, $\Psi_p$ rises, cell firm and supportive; the wall stops it bursting.
**Flaccid (isotonic surroundings):** no net movement, membrane not pressing on wall, tissue limp.
**Plasmolysed (hypertonic surroundings):** water leaves, membrane pulls away from the wall; can be lethal.

sections.content: Animal cells: no wall, no safety net

Animal cells face the same water-potential gradients but lack a cell wall, so nothing generates a rising pressure potential to halt the flow. In a HYPOTONIC solution (higher $\Psi$ outside) water floods in and the cell swells and may burst — lysis, or haemolysis for a red blood cell. In a HYPERTONIC solution (lower $\Psi$ outside) water leaves and the cell shrinks and shrivels (crenation in a red blood cell). Only in an ISOTONIC solution, where inside and outside water potentials are equal, is there no net movement and the cell keeps its normal shape. This is exactly why the water potential of blood and tissue fluid is kept tightly regulated, and why cells for transfusion or culture are suspended in isotonic solutions.

items: **Hypotonic (higher $\Psi$ outside):** water enters; animal cell swells and may burst (lysis).
**Hypertonic (lower $\Psi$ outside):** water leaves; animal cell shrinks / shrivels (crenation).
**Isotonic (equal $\Psi$):** no net movement; cell keeps its shape.
The absence of a cell wall means nothing stops inflow before the cell bursts — unlike a plant cell.

sections.content: Predicting the direction of net water movement

To predict where water will go between any two regions — two cells, a cell and a solution, or two solutions — work each one's total water potential from $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$, then compare. Water moves from the higher (less negative) $\Psi$ to the lower (more negative) $\Psi$. State the direction explicitly as 'from X to Y' and justify it by naming which value is higher. The two worked examples below show this in action, including the calculation-and-direction combination that D2.3 exam questions love.

sections.question: Cell P has a solute potential of −700 kPa and a pressure potential of +250 kPa. It is placed in a large open beaker of solution whose solute potential is −300 kPa. Calculate the water potential of the cell and of the solution, and predict the direction of net water movement. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — water potential of cell P.**
$\Psi_P = \Psi_s + \Psi_p = -700 + 250 = -450$ kPa.  [M1: correct use of $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$; A1: −450 kPa]

**Step 2 — water potential of the solution.**
The beaker is open, so its pressure potential is 0.
$\Psi_{sol} = \Psi_s + \Psi_p = -300 + 0 = -300$ kPa.  [M1: recognises $\Psi_p = 0$ in an open solution]

**Step 3 — compare and predict.**
$-300$ kPa is higher (less negative) than $-450$ kPa, so water moves from the solution (higher $\Psi$) into the cell (lower $\Psi$).  [A1: correct direction with justification]

*The cell would take in water and become more turgid until its rising $\Psi_p$ lifts $\Psi_P$ up to −300 kPa, at which point net movement stops.*

sections.question: Two adjacent cells in a leaf are in contact. Cell M has a water potential of −520 kPa and cell N has a water potential of −380 kPa. State and explain the direction of net water movement between them. [2]
sections.solution:
**Compare the values.**
−380 kPa (cell N) is higher (less negative) than −520 kPa (cell M).  [M1: identifies N as the higher water potential]

**State the direction.**
Net water moves from cell N (−380 kPa) to cell M (−520 kPa), i.e. from higher to lower water potential.  [A1: correct direction from N to M]

*It does not matter that both values are negative — only the comparison matters. Water always flows toward the more negative value.*

sections.content: In direction questions, never leave the answer as a bare 'from higher to lower'. Name the actual regions and their values ('from N at −380 kPa to M at −520 kPa') and, in calculation questions, write the equation, substitute with signs, then state the result. Marks are lost far more often for an unjustified direction than for arithmetic.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Getting the direction backwards** — writing that water moves to the HIGHER water potential. Water moves to the LOWER (more negative) $\Psi$. This single sign error is the most heavily penalised mistake in D2.3.
**Confusing 'higher' with 'bigger magnitude'** — −350 kPa is higher than −500 kPa. Treating −500 as 'higher' because 500 > 350 flips the answer.
**Making solute potential positive** — $\Psi_s$ is ALWAYS negative (or zero). A positive solute potential is not possible; adding solute can only lower water potential.
**Dropping the minus sign in $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$** — e.g. writing −500 + 150 = 650 instead of −350. The signs carry the physics; lose them and the direction is meaningless.

**Confusing turgid with plasmolysed** — turgid means full and firm after gaining water (hypotonic surroundings); plasmolysed means the membrane has pulled off the wall after losing water (hypertonic surroundings). They are opposite outcomes.
**Claiming a plant cell bursts** — the cell wall generates a pressure potential that stops net inflow, so plant cells do not burst; only wall-less animal cells lyse.
**Answering direction questions with 'water goes where it is more concentrated' alone** — restate it in water-potential terms and name the regions and values, or the explanation mark is not awarded.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

D2.3 questions pair a short calculation with a stated, justified direction, and our marking engine awards them analytically — each distinct valid point is a separate mark. Method marks (M) credit correct reasoning or a correct set-up, answer marks (A) credit a correct value or conclusion, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means a wrong number early on does not cost you the marks that follow, provided your method is written down. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific, named step rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Cell A has a water potential of −400 kPa and neighbouring cell B has a water potential of −650 kPa. State and explain the direction of net water movement, and calculate the water potential of a cell with solute potential −500 kPa and pressure potential +150 kPa. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Compare the two water potentials: −400 kPa (cell A) is higher, i.e. less negative, than −650 kPa (cell B). Water moves from higher water potential to lower water potential, so net water movement is from cell A (−400 kPa) to cell B (−650 kPa). For the second cell, using $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p = -500 + 150 = -350$ kPa.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **M1 (method):** compares the two water potentials / identifies that water moves from higher (less negative) to lower (more negative) water potential.
- **A1 (answer):** states net movement is from A to B, with reasoning that −400 kPa is higher than −650 kPa.
- **M1 (method):** correct use of $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$ with the values substituted (−500 + 150).
- **A1 (answer):** $\Psi = -350$ kPa (correct value and sign).

**Equivalents and ECF accepted.** The engine accepts equivalent phrasings — 'from the less negative to the more negative', 'down the water-potential gradient from A to B', or working shown in MPa (−0.35 MPa) — and applies error-carried-forward: if a student mis-adds to, say, −650 kPa but shows $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$ correctly set up, the method mark still stands. Likewise, a correct direction based on a candidate's own (even wrong) earlier values earns the direction marks.

**What the engine rejects.** Stating 'from B to A' or 'toward the higher water potential' scores zero for direction — the sign is the physics. A bare '−350' with no equation, or an answer that drops the minus sign to give +650, loses the corresponding mark because the reasoning is not creditable.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, (1) compare the $\Psi$ values, (2) state A→B with the higher-to-lower justification, (3) write $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$ with substitution, and (4) give −350 kPa. Each line stands on its own.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: compare the water potentials, name the direction as 'from A to B' with the higher-to-lower reason, show $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$ substituted, and give the final value with its sign. Showing each step is exactly what protects your score.
Where this leads

Water potential is the engine behind water transport across the whole plant: a gradient of steadily falling $\Psi$ runs from moist soil, through the root and up the xylem under tension, to the leaf air spaces and out into a dry atmosphere with an extremely negative water potential. The same signed-number reasoning underlies osmoregulation in animals, the design of intravenous and rehydration fluids, and why salting food or over-fertilising soil draws water out of cells. Master the habit — work each region's $\Psi = \Psi_s + \Psi_p$, compare, and let water fall to the more negative value — and you can predict water movement in any system you meet.
